The '''Libya national football team''' ({{langx|ar|منتخب لِيْبيَا لِكُرَّةُ الْقَدَم}}) represents [[Libya]] in men's international [[association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Libyan Football Federation]]. The team has never qualified for [[FIFA World Cup]] but has qualified for editions of the [[Africa Cup of Nations]] in [[1982 Africa Cup of Nations|1982]], [[2006 Africa Cup of Nations|2006]] and [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations|2012]]. In 1982, the team was both the host and runner-up. In the [[FIFA Arab Cup|Arab Cup]], Libya finished second in [[1964 Arab Cup|1964]] and [[2012 Arab Cup|2012]], and third in [[1966 Arab Cup|1966]]. The team is affiliated with both [[FIFA]] and [[Confederation of African Football]] (CAF).

Due to political circumstances, Libya has typically been less successful in international competition compared to other North African teams like [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]], [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]], [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] and [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]]. Libya has never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]] and its participation in AFCON is sporadic, having only qualified for three AFCON editions.

In the 2010s, Libya's global ranking improved due to the increasing number of Libyan players playing in foreign leagues. At the [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations]], the team recorded their first-ever win in the tournament outside Libya. Their FIFA world ranking rose to a high of 36 in September 2012; Libya then won a gold medal in the [[2014 African Nations Championship]]. However, the [[Libyan Civil War (2014–2020)|Libyan Civil War]] caused the stoppage of the [[Libyan Premier League]] and severely disrupted domestic affairs. Libya was eliminated in the first round of the [[2015 Africa Cup of Nations qualification]] by [[Rwanda national football team|Rwanda]] and failed to qualify for the [[2016 African Nations Championship]] as the defending champions.

==History==

===Early history===
Libya's national team was first initiated in 1918, but did not play an official international until 3 August 1953, when they defeated [[Palestine national football team|Palestine]] 5–2 in the first [[Arab Games]] in 1953. The team's first manager was [[Masoud Zantouny]], and the first foreign manager was Englishman [[James Bingham (footballer)|James Bingham]], who took charge of the Libyan national team for the [[1961 Arab Games]]. The first player ever to score for the Libyan national team in an official international was [[Mukhtar Ghonaay]].

The first penalty ever scored by a member of the national team was in the [[1953 Arab Games]] group stage; in the match against Egypt, [[Ali Zantouny]] scored in the 3–2 defeat. The national team's first participation in the [[Arab Cup]] was in [[1964 Arab Cup|1964]], the second edition of the competition, held in [[Kuwait]].

The first ever player to score for the Libyan national team in a non-official international was [[Mustapha Makki]] in a warm-up friendly played prior to the [[1953 Arab Games]] tournament, played against Palestine in Alexandria in 1952. The national team's first attempt to qualify for an Olympic football tournament was in 1967, where they played their first qualification match against [[Niger national football team|Niger]] in an attempt to qualify for the [[Football at the 1968 Summer Olympics|1968 Olympic football tournament]] in Mexico City.

===World Cups===
Libya first entered the [[FIFA World Cup]] qualifiers in [[1970 FIFA World Cup|1970]]. Their early attempts failed, but during the 1980s the national side strengthened. The country's [[geopolitics|geopolitical]] position, however, affected the football team, who had to withdraw from qualifying for the [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]] and [[1990 FIFA World Cup|1990]] World Cups.

Libya came closest to qualifying for the World Cup in 1986. They came to within a game of reaching the finals in Mexico. After winning their match against [[Sudan national football team|Sudan]] in their first game, the Libyans beat [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]] in the next round before taking on [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]] for a place at the finals. Morocco won the first game 3–0 and went through, even though Libya won the return leg 1–0.

After not entering the 1994 and 1998 FIFA World Cup competition, Libya came back in the qualifying competition for Korea/Japan. The Libyans advanced to the second round at the expense of [[Mali national football team|Mali]], who were beaten 4–3 on aggregate. In the group stage, Libya managed only two draws in eight games.

In the qualifying for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup]], a 9–0 two-legged victory against [[São Tomé and Príncipe national football team|São Tome and Principe]] put the Libyans through to the group stage. Libyan player [[Al-Saadi Gaddafi]] was banned from the team after failing a [[drug test]].

A difficult group followed containing [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]], [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]] and [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]], the eventual group winners and qualifiers for the World Cup. However, The Knights were able to secure good results against these sides, as they beat [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] 2–1 in Tripoli, and held [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]] and [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]] to 0–0 draws, helping them to a 4th-place finish and a place at the [[2006 African Cup of Nations]] finals in [[Egypt]].

During the qualifying campaign for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup]], Libya defeated each side in the second round during home matches (they also defeated [[Lesotho national football team|Lesotho]] away). However they were defeated by Gabon in an away match, and failed to qualify to the next round on goal difference.

In the qualifying campaign for the [[2014 FIFA World Cup]], Libya reached the final match in the group stage without a defeat. They were defeated 1–0 by Cameroon and failed to advance to the final round.

In the qualifying campaign for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup]], Libya defeated [[Rwanda national football team|Rwanda]] 4–1 on aggregate in the second round but were eliminated after losing the first three matches in the group stages.

===African Cup Of Nations===

====Libya 1982====
The biggest football tournament to be held in Libya was the [[1982 African Cup of Nations]]. Libya qualified automatically as hosts and were put in a group alongside Ghana, Cameroon and [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]]. The opening match of the tournament saw the hosts take on [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]] in [[Tripoli, Libya|Tripoli]] in a 2–2 draw. A 2–0 win over Tunisia and a goalless draw against Cameroon saw Libya topping the group.

In the semi-finals, Libya came from behind to beat [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]] 2–1 and set up another match with Ghana, this time in the final on 19 March. Ghana scored first in the 35th minute, but Libya equalised in the 70th. This was followed by a tense period of [[extra time]] in which no goals were scored. In a long [[Penalty shootout (association football)|penalty shootout]], Ghana came out triumphant 7–6.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.pulse.com.gh/bi/sports/the-last-time-how-ghana-managed-an-unlikely-ascension-unto-the-african-football-throne-id6394851.html|title=The Last Time: How Ghana managed an unlikely ascension unto the African football throne|first=Fiifi|last=Anaman|date=19 March 2017|access-date=12 July 2017|archive-date=20 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180620180710/http://www.pulse.com.gh/bi/sports/the-last-time-how-ghana-managed-an-unlikely-ascension-unto-the-african-football-throne-id6394851.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

====Egypt 2006====
Libya's second African Cup of Nations saw a return to the higher levels of the international footballing scene at the [[2006 African Cup of Nations]] finals in [[Egypt]]. They qualified for the competition after a goalless draw with [[Sudan national football team|Sudan]] in their ninth qualifying match.

Libya were drawn in Group A with [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] (the hosts and eventual winners), [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup-qualifiers]] [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]] and [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]]. Libya lost 3–0 to Egypt in Cairo, then lost 2–1 to Ivory Coast. A goalless draw against [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]] saw Libya finish bottom of the group.

===Post-Gaddafi era===

[[File:Libye - Algérie4.jpg|thumb|200px|The national football team of Libya versus Algeria in 2013]]
Libya played its first match after the [[Battle of Tripoli (2011)|Battle of Tripoli]] (and thus the end of the [[History of Libya under Muammar Gaddafi|Gaddafi era]] in Libya) on 3 September 2011, with a new uniform sporting the [[National Transitional Council]] flag of Libya.

The match, part of the [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations qualification]] campaign, resulted in a 1–0 victory over [[Mozambique national football team|Mozambique]]. The historic goal was scored by [[Rabee'a al Laafi]]. Like Libya's previous home match, a 3–0 defeat of [[Comoros national football team|Comoros]] in qualifying, played in [[Stade 26 mars]] in Bamako, Mali, a relocation was necessary due to the ongoing [[2011 Libyan Civil War|Libyan Civil War]], and so the [[Petro Sport Stadium]] in Cairo, Egypt became the venue. The match was played [[Behind closed doors (football)|behind closed doors]] for security reasons.<ref>4 September 2011, [https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-africa-14778283 Libyan football enters post-Gaddafi era] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181031171602/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-africa-14778283 |date=2018-10-31 }}, ''[[BBC News Online]]'', Accessed September 5, 2011.</ref>

Prior to the team's final game in the qualification campaign, against [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]], coach Marcos Paquetá claimed that the team was now "not only playing for football success but for a new government and a new country".<ref>7 October 2011, [https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/15220276 Libya eye unlikely qualification] , ''[[BBC Sport]]'', Accessed October 8, 2011.</ref> The match was played on 8 October 2011, and resulted in a 0–0 draw which was good enough for both teams to qualify. Paquetá and his team danced and celebrated afterwards.<ref>8 October 2011, [https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/15229395 Zambia, Libya make Nations Cup cut] , ''[[BBC Sport]]'', Accessed October 8, 2011.</ref>

In November 2011 the team travelled to the [[United Arab Emirates]] to play a friendly match against Belarus organized by [[FIFA]] and broadcast Dubai Sports. The team members, along with the Libyan national chess team, also attended an event at the Libyan Consulate in Dubai organized to honour their contribution to their country in the field of sports.<ref>29 November 2011, [http://snakhooda.smugmug.com/Other/Libyan-National-Football-Team/20336024_H4bn23#1608573361_BFp4H29 Libyan National Football Team and the Libyan National Chess Team Reception] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120509111026/http://snakhooda.smugmug.com/Other/Libyan-National-Football-Team/20336024_H4bn23#1608573361_BFp4H29 |date=2012-05-09 }}, ''[SmugMug Sohail Nakhooda]'', Accessed 30 November 2011.</ref>

On 7 June 2013, Libya met DR Congo in its first match on home ground in two years.

====2012 Africa Cup of Nations====
Having qualified, Libya were drawn into [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations#Group A|Group A]] with co-hosts [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]], qualification rivals [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]] and pre-tournament favourites [[Senegal national football team|Senegal]].

The Mediterranean Knights' first game, the tournament's opening match, saw them lose to an 87th-minute winner from ex-[[Real Madrid C.F.|Real Madrid]] winger [[Javier Ángel Balboa]]. Libya went on to secure a 2–2 draw with Zambia in terrible conditions at the [[Estadio de Bata]], before two goals from [[Ihaab al Bousseffi]] guided them to a 2–1 victory over Senegal, their first Nations Cup win in 30 years and a first on foreign soil. After four points from three games Libya was eliminated at the group stage.

====2014 African Nations Championship Final====

Libya played Ghana in the 2014 CHAN final. Extra time was given (two 15 minutes), however both teams failed to score. It was taken to penalty shootouts, where the Libyan team scored the first three penalties, missed two others and scored the final sixth and their Ghanaian opponents missed the first two, scored the next three then missed the final sixth penalty (resulting in 3 penalties scored). The match finished (0–0) and was won by the Mediterranean Knights by penalties (4–3).

====Coaching crisis====

After [[Javier Clemente]]'s dismissal in 2016, [[Jalal Damja]] took over the national team. He left in 2017 after his contract expired. [[Omar Almaryami]] was later appointed as coach and led Libya to the semi-finals of the [[2018 African Nations Championship]]. After Libya's elimination by [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]], [[Adel Amrouche]] was appointed in May 2018. His goal was to help Libya qualify for the [[2019 Africa Cup of Nations]]. He led Libya to a 0–0 draw against [[South Africa national football team|South Africa]] away from home. However, days before Libya's match against [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]], Amrouche suddenly left the team's camp and later resigned. During an interview with [[Reuters]], Amrouche said that the reason for his resignation was that the [[Libyan Football Federation]] was repeatedly interfering with his work as a coach. He also cited unpaid wages as a reason for his resignation.

Omar Almaryami was again appointed as a caretaker coach of Libya. The team lost twice to Nigeria (4–0 away, 3–2 home) and Almaryami was replaced by former striker [[Fawzi Al-Issawi]], who led Libya to an 8–1 away win over [[Seychelles national football team|Seychelles]]. However, Libya later lost to South Africa 2–1, and Libya failed to qualify for the 2019 Africa Cup of Nations. It was noted during the match that al-Issawi's assistant, [[Abu Bakr Bani]] was the one who made substitutions and instructed players, leaving many to wonder who was the actual coach.{{citation needed|date=October 2019}}

[[File:كورينتين مارتينز (موريتانيا).jpg|thumb|200px|[[Corentin Martins]] became the manager of the national football team of Libya in 2022]]

After the match against South Africa, [[Jalal Damja]] was reappointed as the head coach for temporary matches in the [[2021 Africa Cup of Nations qualification]] before [[Faouzi Benzarti]] was named as new coach of Libya. Under Benzarti, Libya opened their campaign with a disastrous 1–4 loss to [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]], the home of Benzarti, before managed to salvage an important 2–1 win over [[Tanzania national football team|Tanzania]] to gain hope for qualifying to an AFCON tournament since 2012. Yet, managerial crisis once again erupted when Benzarti left the team and Libya had to appoint a local coach, [[Ali El Margini]], in charge against [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]], a team that had not won a single game in the qualification. Internal instability proved to be a rupture, as Libya lost two consecutive games against the Central African opponent and fell out of top two position. El Margini left after losing all three of his games in charge. He was replaced by [[Zoran Filipović]], who led a team of domestic players into the [[2020 African Nations Championship]]. Libya were eliminated in the group stage after two draws and a loss in three games. Defeats in their final two AFCON qualifiers saw Filipovic sacked in May 2021.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/africa/57254737 |title=Libya looking for a new men's football coach |work=BBC Sport |date=26 May 2021 |access-date=2021-05-31 |archive-date=2021-05-31 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210531050507/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/africa/57254737 |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Javier Clemente]] was reappointed as head coach shortly afterwards.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://eldesmarque.com/bizkaia/athletic-club/noticias/289093-javier-clemente-necesito-entrenar-es-como-un-doping-para-mi |title=Javier Clemente vuelve: "Necesito entrenar, es como un doping para mi" |work=ElDesmarque Bizkaia |date=30 May 2021 |access-date=2021-05-31 |archive-date=2021-05-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210530120428/https://eldesmarque.com/bizkaia/athletic-club/noticias/289093-javier-clemente-necesito-entrenar-es-como-un-doping-para-mi |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://libyareview.com/13200/javier-clemente-libyan-national-football-teams-new-coach/ |title=Javier Clemente: Libyan National Football Team's New Coach |date=22 May 2021 |access-date=2021-05-31 |archive-date=2021-05-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210523075639/https://libyareview.com/13200/javier-clemente-libyan-national-football-teams-new-coach/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Kits==
{{Commons|Libya national football team kits}}
In the [[Gaddafi]] era the National team used to play its home matches wearing the green coloured kit representing the Flag of [[Libyan Arab Jamahiriya]]. However, after the [[First Libyan Civil War|Libyan Civil War]] in 2011, Libya changed its flag to the [[Flag of Libya|new one]] which was used from 1951 to 1969 back when Libya was a Kingdom. This change resulted in changing the national team's kit in order to represent the new flag.  The team played its home matches with colours: Red, Black and Green (as in the flag). Red dominates the strip and is the sole jersey colour.  The [[away colours]] were white in both eras. Since 2011, the LFF emblem and the national team's badge was changed into the current design. The previous badge was two balls in front of green coloured Libya's map which is also in front of a sun.

During late 2011 and early 2012 the Libyan team wore white jerseys temporarily in their qualification games and [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations]]. However, in mid-2012 the team began to use red jerseys.
In 2014, Libya replaced the green socks worn by the players with black ones.

[[Adidas]] is the supplier of the official team strip.

==Home stadium==
{{Main|Tripoli Stadium}}
[[File:11 June Stadium BenTaher.jpg|thumb|left|175px|Tripoli Stadium]]
The Tripoli Stadium is a [[multi-purpose stadium]] in [[Tripoli, Libya|Tripoli]], [[Libya]]. It can hold 80,000 spectators.

It was the main venue used by the Libyan national football team in its FIFA World Cup and African Nations Cup qualifying matches as well as friendlies and other international games.

The stadium hosted many games of the [[1982 African Cup of Nations]] held in Libya along with the [[28 March Stadium]] in [[Benghazi]].

The [[28 March Stadium]] in Benghazi was also used by the national team sometimes.

FIFA lifted the ban on Libyan stadiums in 2013, during the [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification]]. However, it was re-imposed in 2014 due to increased security concerns. The Libyan national team was forced to host games in neighboring countries such as [[Algeria]], [[Morocco]], [[Egypt]] or [[Tunisia]] (Tunisia the most popular choice due to its close distance to Libya).

Libya played their first home game since 2013 at the [[Martyrs of February Stadium]] in [[Benghazi]] against [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] on 25 March 2021, where they lost 2–5.

==Rivalries==
Libya's only real rivalries are with its fellow [[Maghreb|North African]] footballing nations, [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]], [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]], [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] and, mainly, [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]]. Matches between Libya and any one of these opponents are highly charged encounters. Libya defeated Egypt 2–1 in a World Cup qualifier on 8 October 2004, the [[Egypt national football team|Pharaohs]] only managed to beat the Libyans on their own turf twice. The rivalry was rekindled at the [[Football at the 2007 Arab Games|2007 Arab Games]], where the teams drew 0–0; Egypt eventually claimed the gold medal on goal difference from the Libyans.

Libya also has a rivalry with [[Morocco national football team|Morocco]]. Libya's last win against Morocco was during the 1986 World Cup qualifiers, which Libya won 1–0.  A friendly was played between both countries on the 11th of October 2019 in which they tied. Matches between Libya and [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] are also very tense, the last time they played was a 5–2 win from the latter in the African Cup of Nations qualification group stage round.

==Results and fixtures==

*[[Michel Termanini|Termanini]] {{penmiss}}
*[[Amid Mahajna|Mahajna]] {{pengoal}}

|result=D
}}

==Coaching history==

{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
*{{flagicon|Libya|1951}} [[:fr:Massoud Zantouny|Massoud Zantouny]] (1953)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1951}} [[Salim Faraj Balteb]] (1957–1960)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[James Benjeham]] (1961)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[Billy Elliott (footballer)|Billy Elliott]] (1961–1963)
*{{flagicon|Yugoslavia}} [[Vojin Božović]] (1964–1965)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[George Skinner (footballer)|George Skinner]] (1965–1966)
*{{flagicon|Algeria}} [[:fr:Mokhtar Arribi|Mokhtar Arribi]] (1966–1967)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[Keith Spurgeon]] (1967–1968)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1951}} [[:fr:Ali Zantouny|Ali Zantouny]] (1968–1969)
*{{flagicon|Yugoslavia}} [[Milan Selbetishi]] (1969–1970)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[George Ainsley]] (1970–1971)
*{{flagicon|Romania|1965}} [[Nicolae Oaidă]] (1971–1972)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1972}} [[Hassan Al-Amer]] (1972)
*{{flagicon|Romania|1965}} [[Titus Ozon]] (1972–1974)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1972}} [[:ar:محمد الخمسي|Mohammed El-Khamisi]] (1974–1975)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1972}} [[Abed Ali Al-Aqili]] (1975–1976)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1972}} [[:ar:محمد الخمسي|Mohammed El-Khamisi]] (1976–1977)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[Ali Al-Zaqori]] (1977–1978)
*{{flagicon|England}} [[Ron Bradley (football manager)|Ron Bradley]] (1978–1980)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[:ar:محمد الخمسي|Mohammed El-Khamisi]] (1980–1982)
*{{flagicon|Hungary}} [[Béla Gutal]] (1982)
*{{flagicon|Romania|1965}} [[Cicerone Manolache]] (1983–1984)<ref>[http://www.libertatea.ro/detalii/articol/ca-selectioner-al-libiei-cicerone-manolache-avea-un-salariu-de-2-000-dar-statul-roman-oprea-1-700-328558.html Ca selecţioner al Libiei, Cicerone Manolache avea un salariu de 2.000 $, dar statul român oprea 1.700 $] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110907094237/http://www.libertatea.ro/detalii/articol/ca-selectioner-al-libiei-cicerone-manolache-avea-un-salariu-de-2-000-dar-statul-roman-oprea-1-700-328558.html |date=2011-09-07 }}, www.libertatea.ro, 29 mars 2011.</ref>
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[:ar:محمد الخمسي|Mohammed El-Khamisi]] (1984)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[Hashimi El-Bahlul]] (1984–1986)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[:ar:محمد الخمسي|Mohammed El-Khamisi]] (1988–1989)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[Ahmed Ben Soueid]] (1989)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[Hashimi El-Bahlul]] (1991–1997)
*{{flagicon|Romania}} [[Ion Moldovan]] (1998)
*{{flagicon|Scotland}} [[Danny McLennan]] (1998)
*{{flagicon|Italy}} [[Eugenio Bersellini]] (1998–1999)
*{{flagicon|Argentina|1861}} [[Carlos Bilardo]] (1999–2000)
*{{flagicon|Argentina|1861}} [[:es:Miguel Ángel Lemme|Miguel Angel Lemme]] (2000–2001)
*{{flagicon|Italy}} [[Francesco Scoglio]] (2002)
*{{flagicon|Croatia}} [[Ilija Lončarević]] (2003–2004)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[:ar:محمد الخمسي|Mohammed El-Khamisi]] (2004–2005)
*{{flagicon|Croatia}} [[Ilija Lončarević]] (2005–2006)
*{{flagicon|Egypt}} [[Mohsen Saleh]] (2006)
*{{flagicon|Libya|1977}} [[Abou Bakr Bani]] (2006–2007)
*{{flagicon|Tunisia}} [[Faouzi Benzarti]] (2007–2009)
*{{flagicon|Serbia}} [[Branko Smiljanić]] (2009–2010)
*{{flagicon|Brazil}} [[Marcos Paquetá]] (2010–2012)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Abdul-Hafeedh Arbeesh]] (2012–2013)
*{{flagicon|Spain}} [[Javier Clemente]] (2013–2016)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Jalal Damja]] (2016–2017)
*{{flagicon|Algeria}} [[Adel Amrouche]] (2017–2018)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Fawzi Al-Issawi]] (2018–2019)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Jalal Damja]] (2019)
*{{flagicon|Tunisia}} [[Faouzi Benzarti]] (2019–2020)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Ali El Margini]] (2020)
*{{flagicon|Montenegro}} [[Zoran Filipović]] (2020–2021)
*{{flagicon|Spain}} [[Javier Clemente]] (2021–2022)
*{{flagicon|Spain}} [[Ramon Catala]] (2022)
*{{flagicon|France}} [[Corentin Martins]] (2022–2023)<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/africa/64721145 |title=Libya search for homegrown coach as Corentin Martins departs |last=Dakhil |first=Morad |work=BBC Sport |date=February 21, 2023 |access-date=February 23, 2023 |archive-date=February 23, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230223135557/https://www.bbc.com/sport/africa/64721145 |url-status=live }}</ref>
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Hamdi Bataw]] (2023)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Salim Al-Jalali]] (interim, 2023)
*{{flagicon|Serbia}} [[Milutin Sredojević]] (2023–2024)
*{{flagicon|Libya}} [[Nasser Al-Hadrininew]] (interim, 2024–2025)
*{{flagicon|Senegal}} [[Aliou Cissé]] (2025–2026)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following 28 players were called up for the [[Exhibition game#Association football|friendly]] matches against [[Niger national football team|Niger]] and [[Liberia national football team|Liberia]] on 27 and 31 March 2026.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.facebook.com/Libya1.LFF/posts/pfbid0junf9Zcndzo1cXmnF3t9YiEvTQQcHQef9yhN9gepGQ1faqeNzL7UUEQNxELJg1nVl|title=القائمة الرسمية للاعبين المستدعين للتوقف الدولي لشهر مارس 2026|trans-title=The official list of players called up for the March 2026 international window|publisher=[[Libyan Football Federation]]|access-date=18 March 2026|language=ar|via=[[Facebook]]}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://libyareview.com/64112/cisse-announces-libya-squad-for-march-international-break/|title=Cissé Announces Libya Squad for March International Break|publisher=[[Libya Review]]|date=19 March 2026}}</ref>

''Caps and goals correct as of 25 March 2025, after the match against [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]]''.

{{nat fs g start}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up to the Libya squad in the last 12 months.

{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury
*<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby
*<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew due to non-injury issue
{{nat fs end}}

*<small>Football at the Summer Olympics has been an under-23 tournament since the 1992 edition.</small>

!Total||Group stage||1/11||3||1||0||2||3||4
|}

* <small>Prior to the [[1991 All-Africa Games|Cairo 1991]] campaign, the [[Football at the All-Africa Games]] was open to full senior national teams.</small>
* {{note|n1|1}} <small>Libya was disqualified from the tournament due to violence with the Egyptian team during the match in the group stage.</small>

===African Nations Championship===
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
|-
!colspan=9|[[African Nations Championship]] record
|-
!colspan=9|Appearances: 4{{refn|Libya played against [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] at the [[2020 African Nations Championship qualification]], home and away, and Libya lost both matches 0–1 and 1–2. On 20 December 2019, the [[Tunisian Football Federation]] withdrew from the finals due to the pressure of the calendar after the approval of the clubs. On 31 January 2020, the [[Confederation of African Football]] (CAF) decided to replace Tunisia with Libya in the finals.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Football|first=CAF-Confedération Africaine du|title=Libya replaces Tunisia for Total CHAN Cameroon 2020|url=https://www.cafonline.com/news-center/news/libya-replaces-tunisia-for-total-chan-cameroon-2020|access-date=2022-02-15|website=CAFOnline.com|language=en}}</ref>|group="note"}}
|-
!Year
!Round
!Position
!{{Tooltip|Pld|Games played}}
!{{Tooltip|W|Won}}
!{{Tooltip|D|Drawn}}
!{{Tooltip|L|Lost}}
!{{Tooltip|GF|Goals for}}
!{{Tooltip|GA|Goals against}}
|-
|{{flagicon|CIV}} [[2009 African Nations Championship|2009]]||Group stage||7th||3||0||2||1||1||3
|-
|{{flagicon|SUD}} [[2011 African Nations Championship|2011]]||colspan=8|''Did not qualify''
|- style="background:gold;"
|{{flagicon|RSA}} [[2014 African Nations Championship|2014]]||'''Champions'''|| '''1st'''||'''6'''||'''1'''||'''5'''||'''0'''||'''6'''||'''4'''
|-
|{{flagicon|RWA}} [[2016 African Nations Championship|2016]]||colspan=8 |''Did not qualify''
|- style="background:#9acdff;"
|{{flagicon|MAR}} [[2018 African Nations Championship|2018]]||'''Fourth place'''||'''4th'''||'''6'''||'''2'''||'''2'''||'''2'''||'''7'''||'''6'''
|-
|{{flagicon|CMR}} [[2020 African Nations Championship|2020]]|| rowspan=2|Group stage||13th||3||0||2||1||1|||2
|-
|{{flagicon|ALG}} [[2022 African Nations Championship|2022]]|| 9th||3||1||0||2||1|||2
|-
|{{flagicon|KEN}} {{flagicon|TAN}} {{flagicon|UGA}} [[2024 African Nations Championship|2024]]
| colspan="8" |''Withdrew''
|-
!Total||1 title||5/7||21||4||11||6||16||17
|}

*<small>Prior to the [[1991 Mediterranean Games|Athens 1991]] campaign, the [[Football at the Mediterranean Games]] was open to full senior national teams.</small>

===Palestine Cup of Nations===
{{main|Palestine Cup of Nations}}

==Honours==
===Continental===
* '''[[Africa Cup of Nations]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1982 Africa Cup of Nations|1982]]
* '''[[African Nations Championship]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': [[2014 African Nations Championship|2014]]

===Regional===
* '''[[Arab Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[1964 Arab Cup|1964]], [[2012 Arab Cup|2012]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.alarabiya.net/articles/2012%2F07%2F07%2F224913|title=Morocco wins Arab Cup 2012 title|website=alarabiya.net|publisher=Al Arabia News|date=7 July 2012|access-date=26 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211126103808/https://english.alarabiya.net/articles/2012/07/07/224913|archive-date=26 November 2021}}</ref>
**{{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[1966 Arab Cup|1966]]
* '''[[Islamic Games]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (1)''': [[Football at the Islamic Games|1980]]

===Friendly===
* '''[[LG Cup (association football)|LG Cup]] (1)''': 2004
